figures.caption: FIG. 1. Orbital order in BNOO inferred from NMR spectra and bi-modal spectroscopy. (a) Illustration of the phase diagram of BNOO. At low temperatures the sample shows both magnetic long-range order (LRO) and broken local point symmetry (BLPS). The LRO disappears as the sample undergoes a magnetic phase transition at temperature Tc, but the BLPS is still observable in NMR spectra until temperature T ∗ [60]. Above T ∗ the system was characterized as a paramagnet (PM) in previous studies [60]. (b) Representative 23Na spectra in different phases at the temperatures, denoted by stars in the phase diagram (a), and a sketch of the local distortions that give rise to a finite electric field gradient (EFG) (upper inset) [61, 62]. Only one quadrupolar triplet from the two distinct magnetic sites is shown at 5.1 K for clarity. The zero (reference) frequency is defined by ω0 = 23γH of Na nuclei, while the magnetic field is H = 7 T applied parallel to the [001] axis. (c) Spin-echo magnitude as a function of inter-pulse times (2τ , with echo sequence in upper inset). The 5.1 K decay curve is divided into two sections from two different measurements, an attenuating modulation (lower left inset) and an exponential decay. The higher-temperature curves reveal two relaxation processes at different time scales, characterized by a “plateau” at time TQ. Error bars are smaller than the symbols used. (d) Solid symbols denote the spin-echo magnitude, measured from the integrated area of the signal in the frequency domain, as a function of the inter-pulse times (2τ). Solid lines are fits to the theoretical model [63]. The position of the plateau corresponds to the rate T−1 Q . The site frequencies are given by the Lorentzian distribution model shown in (e).

FIG. 2. Order parameters and relaxation processes in BNOO. (a) T dependence of the orbital order parameter mean, ωQ, (a) and its distribution (2/TQ) (b). (b) The magnetic (2/T2) and orbital (2/TQ) distributions causing dephasing, as a function of temperature up to 380 K. The dashed line denotes Tc, the transition temperature below which the LRO magnetic state is formed. The orbital glass state, denoted as OrG, is characterized by the dominant distribution of the orbital order parameter (2/TQ). A nematic orbital glass (NOrG) is stabilized in the LRO magnetic state. All data were measured in a 7 T magnetic field applied parallel to the [001] crystalline axis. When not discernible, error bars are smaller than the symbols used. The open red points are upper estimates using the spectral linewidth (See Suppl.). Thick shaded red and navy lines serve as a guide to the eye. (c) The top panel displays possible in-plane distortions of BNOO’s unit cell. The angle of the distortion is given by θQ and its magnitude by rQ, yielding an order parameter of the form ωQ ≡ rQ(sin θQ, | cos θQ|). The following panels illustrate spatial variations in the BLPS phase above Tc and a global BLPS phase below, as inferred by this work. Each unit cell is represented by a colored rod to indicate the local BLPS structure. The angle of the rod and its hue indicate the local θQ, while the rod’s length and its opacity indicate the local rQ. For visualization purposes, we only show variations in a 2D plane, but in practice the variations should occur in 3D.

FIG. 3. Angle and pulse dependence measurements. (a) Echo area as a function of the delay time 2τ at different tip angles ϕ, at 26 K, fit with Eq. 5. Here, we take A = C as they are close in value around the perfect π/2 pulse [63]. The average value ωQ is zero. (b) Tip-angle (ϕ) dependence of the prefactors in Eq. 5: A,C (associated with exponents of 2τ and 0, labeled even) and B (associated with τ , labeled odd). Data is extracted from the fits given in (a), with one standard deviation given by the error bars. Solid lines are theoretical predictions [63]. (C) Dependence of the rate 2/TQ on the orientation of the sample (θ) between its principal axis VZZ (local EFG) and the applied field H = 7 T, at two temperatures. Solid lines are a fit to Eq. 8, implying a distribution of quadrupole moments. When not discernible, error bars are smaller than the symbols used.
